summary:
The Congress of Vienna, convened from September 1814 to June 1815, was a diplomatic conference of European powers assembled to restructure the political order of Europe in the aftermath of the [[event:109|Napoleonic Wars]]. Hosted in the Habsburg capital and presided over by the Austrian foreign minister Prince Klemens von Metternich, it brought together representatives of all the major European states — most significantly Britain, Austria, Prussia, Russia, and France — to negotiate a comprehensive peace settlement.

The Congress operated on several guiding principles: the restoration of legitimate monarchies, the maintenance of a balance of power that would prevent any single state from dominating Europe, and the suppression of the revolutionary and nationalist forces that had destabilized the continent since 1789. These principles, collectively known as the Concert of Europe, shaped European diplomacy for decades. France, despite being the defeated power, was included as a full participant under the skilled representation of Talleyrand and treated with remarkable generosity — its pre-revolutionary borders were essentially restored.

The territorial settlements were extensive and complex. The German-speaking territories were reorganized into the loosely structured German Confederation of 39 states, replacing the Holy Roman Empire dissolved by Napoleon. Poland was partitioned once more among Russia, Prussia, and Austria, with a nominally autonomous Kingdom of Poland under the Russian Tsar. The Netherlands and Belgium were united into the Kingdom of the United Netherlands. Britain retained several strategic colonial acquisitions including the Cape Colony in South Africa and Malta.

The Congress established the idea of great-power consultation — the Concert of Europe — as a mechanism for managing international disputes. Periodic congresses at Aix-la-Chapelle, Troppau, Laibach, and Verona attempted to coordinate conservative responses to revolutionary movements. While this system eventually broke down under the pressure of liberal nationalism, it represented an early experiment in collective international security that prefigured later multilateral institutions.

The Congress's conservative settlement provoked lasting opposition. The suppression of nationalist aspirations in Germany, Italy, Greece, and Poland generated decades of unrest and revolution, most dramatically in the [[event:2532|Revolutions of 1848]]. The [[event:2533|Italian Unification]] and [[event:2534|German Unification]] movements that transformed Europe in the 1860s and 1870s represented in large part a repudiation of the Vienna settlement's principle that dynastic legitimacy should take precedence over national identity.

Nevertheless, the Congress of Vienna's achievement was remarkable by historical standards. The peace it constructed, however imperfect, avoided a general European war for nearly a century — an unprecedented period of relative great-power stability. The Congress became a model for subsequent multilateral peacemaking, with its methods and principles cited at the Paris Peace Conference of 1919 and studied ever since by diplomats and historians as an example of successful — if ultimately incomplete — conflict resolution.
